intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users want the fastest path to action—showing cook time + key ingredients upfront (numbers + names) rather than full recipe narrative
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data (time, ingredients) vs. narrative story (method, tips)
facade.label: Timer or ingredient list first
facade.acceptImplies: Recipe app leads with constraints and essentials; narrative/personality comes after commitment
facade.rejectImplies: Users want context, story, or method overview before deciding if a recipe fits their mood or skill level

facade.hypothesis: A playful, slightly irreverent tone with conversational personality would resonate with people frustrated by cooking
facade.label: Sassy little tips
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward warm, humanized, anti-establishment voice—embrace casual language, humor about cooking failures, buddy-like energy
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers straightforward, no-nonsense tone—lean into efficient, clarity-focused, respectful-but-neutral voice that doesn't try too hard to entertain

evidence.hypothesis: Users want the fastest path to action—showing cook time + key ingredients upfront (numbers + names) rather than full recipe narrative
evidence.implication: Recipe app leads with constraints and essentials; narrative/personality comes after commitment

facade.hypothesis: A recipe app for people who hate cooking works best with uncluttered, cheerful UI that makes each recipe feel approachable and quick, not intimidating
facade.label: Bright, minimal cards
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers clean layouts with generous whitespace and approachable (not dark or heavy) color palettes—likely values ease-of-use over visual richness
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer darker themes, denser information layouts, or more personality-driven design—suggests they want atmosphere or efficiency over minimalism

facade.hypothesis: A recipe app for people who hate cooking should feel encouraging and approachable rather than intimidating—using warm, friendly visual language that makes the task feel less daunting and more playful.
facade.axisTargeted: warm vs cool + calm vs energetic
facade.label: Cheerful but simple
facade.acceptImplies: The interface should lean into emotional support: bright accents, encouraging copy, friendly typography, and a tone that celebrates small wins. This user needs a mood boost, not clinical precision.
facade.rejectImplies: The app should avoid cold efficiency (stark minimalism, professional chef vibes, clinical layouts). This user doesn't want to feel like they're failing at a serious craft—they want permission to make it easy.

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants a calm, low-pressure environment vs. gamified, achievement-driven experience with rewards
Whether user prioritizes time estimates and difficulty ratings vs. ingredient sourcing and substitution options
Whether user wants instant recipe access vs. animated guidance, progress indicators, and satisfying completion feedback

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants a calm, low-pressure environment vs. gamified, achievement-driven experience with rewards
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes time estimates and difficulty ratings vs. ingredient sourcing and substitution options
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant recipe access vs. animated guidance, progress indicators, and satisfying completion feedback
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer animated modals that slide/fade in over instant overlays, reducing cognitive jarring when adding ingredients
facade.label: Ingredient picker pops up
facade.acceptImplies: Design ingredient selection, quantity adjustments, and substitution flows with smooth entrance animations and layered reveals
facade.rejectImplies: Keep modals, forms, and selection screens appearing instantly without transition — users value speed and directness over motion feedback

facade.hypothesis: Users respond better to visual step-by-step photos than to written ingredient descriptions or technique explanations—especially people intimidated by cooking language
facade.label: Photos over words
facade.acceptImplies: Recipe app should lead with image-first instructional design (photo per step, minimal captions) to lower cognitive load for cooking-averse users
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer reading instructions or don't need visual scaffolding—text-based recipes with optional photos are sufficient

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer single-action interfaces that minimize decision friction—tapping once to start a meal rather than navigating menus or reading instructions.
facade.axisTargeted: Interaction density and cognitive load in the navigation pattern
facade.label: One-tap cooking
facade.acceptImplies: Lean toward big touch targets, auto-play recipe sequences, and dismiss modal flows. Minimize text-heavy menus.
facade.rejectImplies: Users may want to browse, compare, customize, or read ahead before committing. Need visible recipe preview and optional steps.

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers dead-simple meals in under 15 minutes with minimal ingredients and zero fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals so simple, you'll actually make them

Today's Easiest Meals

Garlic Butter Pasta

8 min3 ingredients

Boil pasta. Toss with butter, garlic, and salt. Done. No sauce required.

Egg Fried Rice

10 min5 ingredients

Leftover rice + eggs + soy sauce. Stir in a pan. Eat straight from the pan.

Avocado Toast

3 min2 ingredients

Toast bread. Smash avocado on it. Salt. Pepper. Breakfast solved.

What You'll Need

Pantry Staples

  • Pasta (any shape)
  • Eggs
  • Butter
  • Soy sauce
  • Salt & pepper
  • Garlic (fresh or powder)

Skip the Cooking Stress

Every recipe takes 15 minutes or less. No fancy techniques. No weird ingredients.
